explanation.title: Dynamic Collections with Java's ArrayList
explanation.content:
# Limitations of Arrays

Java arrays have a fixed size — once created, you cannot add or remove elements. If you do not know the final count in advance, or if the collection grows as the program runs, you need a more flexible data structure.

## ArrayList

`ArrayList` is a resizable list from `java.util`. It grows automatically as you add elements:

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

ArrayList<String> names = new ArrayList<>();
names.add("Rahul");
names.add("Priya");
names.add("Amit");
System.out.println(names.size());   // 3
System.out.println(names.get(0));   // Rahul
```

The `<String>` in angle brackets is a **type parameter** — it tells Java what type of objects the list holds.

## Common ArrayList Methods

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| `.add(element)` | Appends to the end |
| `.add(index, element)` | Inserts at position |
| `.get(index)` | Returns element at index |
| `.set(index, element)` | Replaces element at index |
| `.remove(index)` | Removes by index |
| `.remove(object)` | Removes first occurrence of object |
| `.size()` | Returns count of elements |
| `.contains(element)` | Returns true if element present |
| `.indexOf(element)` | Returns first index or -1 |
| `.clear()` | Removes all elements |
| `.isEmpty()` | Returns true if size is 0 |

## Iterating Over an ArrayList

```java
// Enhanced for — most readable
for (String name : names) {
    System.out.println(name);
}

// Classic for — use when you need the index
for (int i = 0; i < names.size(); i++) {
    System.out.println(i + ": " + names.get(i));
}
```

## ArrayList vs Array — When to Use Which

| Situation | Use |
|---|---|
| Fixed size, known at creation | `int[]` array |
| Size changes at runtime | `ArrayList<Integer>` |
| Primitive performance critical | Array |
| Need .contains(), .indexOf() | ArrayList |

In the code example below, you will see an ArrayList managing a dynamic student roster with add, remove, and search operations.

codeExample.title: Dynamic Student Roster
codeExample.explanation:
- `ArrayList<String> roster = new ArrayList<>()` — the type parameter `<String>` ensures only Strings can be added
- `.add("Kavitha Nair")` — appends to the end; the list grows automatically
- `.remove("Amit Patel")` — removes the first matching element; returns `true` if found
- `.indexOf(search)` — returns the zero-based position; `idx + 1` converts to 1-based for display
- `for (String name : roster)` — the enhanced for loop works with ArrayList just like arrays
- `.startsWith("P")` — String method used inside the loop to filter matching elements

exercise.title: Build a Course Enrolment Manager
exercise.instructions:
Create an ArrayList<String> named courses. Add these four courses: 'Python', 'JavaScript', 'Java', 'HTML/CSS'. Print the count using size(). Check if 'Java' is in the list using contains() and print the result. Remove 'Python' and print the updated list using a for-each loop. Add 'React' and print the final count.

Expected output includes size 4, Java present true, Python removed, and final size 4.
hints: 💡 Add a for-each loop: for (String c : courses) { System.out.println("  " + c); }
💡 After adding React, print: System.out.println("Final count: " + courses.size());
💡 ArrayList uses .size() (not .length) to return the number of elements.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of ArrayList over a regular Java array?
options: ArrayList holds primitive types directly; arrays require wrappers
ArrayList can grow and shrink dynamically; arrays have a fixed size set at creation
ArrayList is faster than arrays for all operations
ArrayList can hold multiple types simultaneously; arrays cannot
questions.explanation: A Java array has a fixed size determined when it is created with new int[5] — you cannot add or remove elements. ArrayList dynamically resizes as you call add() and remove(). This makes ArrayList ideal when the number of elements is not known in advance or changes during program execution.

questions.question: How do you access the element at index 2 in an ArrayList named list?
questions.explanation: ArrayList elements are accessed with the .get(index) method. Unlike arrays (which use arr[index] bracket notation), ArrayList requires a method call. list.get(2) returns the element at zero-based index 2. Attempting bracket notation on an ArrayList causes a compile error.
